comments.content:
It's getting on for midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"Thus disposed, the Tarot is a true oracle and responds to all questions with more clarity and infallibility than the android of Albertus Magnus, to such an extent that a prisoner without any books could, in a few years, if he had only a Tarot deck and knew how to use it, acquire universal science, and would speak of everything with a doctrine without equal and with an inexhaustible eloquence.  This wheel is the true key to the notory art and the Great Art of Raymond Lully; it is the true secret of the transmutation of darkness into light, it is the first and most important of all the arcana of the Great Work." 

That's from Eliphas Lévi's The Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic, in case you were wondering -- and yes, Lévi used the word "android." (The word comes from Greek, androeides, "image of a man;" the feminine equivalent is gynoeides, and thus "gynoid" would be the word in English for a female robot. You're welcome.)  

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***

replies.content: Really, really basic question: how do you pray?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Praying is talking to gods. Unless you're a Christian, I don't recommend the on-your-knees posture -- that has very negative connotations for a lot of people these days. In the classical world, people prayed standing, though you can also do it seated. Settle yourself in a stable position, turn your attention toward the deity you want to pray to, and talk. Do it as though you have the chance to talk to a wise and powerful elder. Don't grovel, don't flatter, don't whine; explain why you want to pray to the deity, ask for what you intend to ask for, thank the deity for listening. Then -- and this is crucial -- be silent for a while. Listen for an answer. You may not get one; you may feel an answer -- an emotional tone, say -- or you may have words surface in your mind; but it's at least as important to listen to deities as it is to speak to them.

Did you ever watch Monty Python and the Holy Grail? One of the scenes always seemed to me to offer good advice...

GOD: Arthur! Arthur, King of the Britons! Oh, don't grovel! If there's one thing I can't stand, it's people groveling.

ARTHUR: Sorry--

GOD: And don't apologize. Every time I try to talk to someone it's "sorry this" and "forgive me that" and "I'm not worthy". What are you doing now!?

ARTHUR: I'm averting my eyes, oh Lord.

GOD: Well, don't.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The history of Lilith is long and weird. She was originally a second Moon; then some European astrologers applied the same name and symbolism to the focus of the Moon's orbit that the Earth doesn't occupy. (A circle has a center, an ellipse has two foci, and in an elliptical orbit, the body that's being orbited is always at one of those two foci.) There's disagreement between those who use the actual position of the second focus and those who use its mean positio. Then there's an asteroid named Lilith, and some people use that in place of the others!

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

1. I've been meditating in the concept of the will lately, and I'm extremely intrigued by the concept that willing a single thing strongly for an extended period of time encourages that thing to manifest in our lives. If this is remotely true, it would seem to be one of the most important lessons we could possibly learn. In particular, what I find fascinating is the idea that internal struggle is a symptom of divided will, and that if the will is properly directed, there is no sense of resistance at all - this seems to turn a lot of pop psychology and contemporary self-improvement on their heads, since they're all about fighting yourself to "build willpower." However, what I don't understand is how the will is different from simply desiring or wanting something and working towards achieving it. How can we move from a want or desire for something to a will that can get the job done without internal resistance or self-sabotage? Subjectively, does the will feel differently from a desire or want?

2. Are you familiar with Peter Turchin and his academic work to develop mathematical models of history? Back in 2010, he successfully predicted a wave of instability in the US in the decade 2010-2020 (it seems pretty obvious now for those who have been paying attention, but hindsight is 20/20). Are you sympathetic to such ideas in general? Can history be modeled like an epidemic?

3. In the Qabalah, what is the difference between the forms and patterns of Hod and the images of Yesod? I'm guessing that since Yesod proceeds from Hod in the traditional order of emanations, its images are shaped by the patterns of Hod, but how do we know in practice if we're operating in the sphere of Hod or Yesod?

4. Is any distinction made in occult philosophy between feeling and emotion? Jung called feeling a judging function, and I get the impression that an emotion is something more physiological and automatic. Would you agree with this? Which Sephiroth correspond to feeling and emotion, respectively?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The crucial thing here is to learn to separate the will from wants and desires, because these always involve conflict. To will something is to choose it, not because you want it or crave it or have some other emotional reaction pushing you toward it, but simply because you have freely chosen it. Fighting yourself to build willpower is a mistake -- you build willpower by doing things "just because." Right now, decide to get up, cross the room, and touch a particular point on the far wall. Then do it. That's a simple act of will -- and a basic training exercise -- because you had no reason to do it except your act of free choice.

2) I'm somewhat familiar with Turchin, and I have to say I have more respect for his models than I did before! History can certainly be modeled, though I tend to use narrative rather than numerical models; Oswald Spengler is a writer on historical cycles who's influenced me strongly.

3) An image isn't just a pattern. It's a pattern that expresses itself in mental equivalents of the physical senses -- think of seeing something "in the mind's eye," and so on. Those belong to Yesod. The patterns of Hod are not expressed in images; they're like the field of a magnet, which you can't see until you sprinkle iron filings onto the paper and they fall into lines that trac the patterns.

4) Words like those are tricky! The term "feeling" can refer to anything from intuitive flickers of the spiritual level to hardwired biological passions -- and so can the term "emotion," depending on who's using them. As a rule, the biological passions belong to Yesod, the location of the automatic consciousness, while what you're calling "feeling" belongs to Netzach.

replies.content:
I've never met anyone who actually lacked willpower. The problem is usually that they've gotten into the habit of willing two or more incompatible things all the time, and that makes it impossible for them to use their willpower.

To deal with procrastination, start by understanding it. Journaling is a great tool here. Very often the habit of procrastination has complex emotional roots. For example, I used to have a helluva time finishing any project. That was because all through my childhood, since I was labeled "gifted," I was constantly being loaded with extreme expectations about how I would perform in school or whatever; when I managed to live up to those, that was treated as nothing special, the sort of thing I should achieve all the time, while when I couldn't live up to those, I got it in the neck. So I embraced failure as a way to fight back. Later on, I had to break that habit, and that took quite a bit of journaling and reflection, bringing up bad memories, etc.

Schools and society did indeed teach willpower; here's a link to a PDF of Charles Godfrey Leland's fine training manual on the subject. As for why that's become taboo, exactly; as the old Stoics taught, if you can rule yourself, no one else can rule you.

replies.title: Re: Hellenic magic/worship
replies.content:
The traditional lore gives some suggestions. You should get a statue of her if you can, and set up a shrine to her -- it can be on top of a dresser or bookcase, or what have you -- involving the statue, a cloth between the statue and the dresser or bookcase top, an incense burner of some kind, and a small plate used only for this purpose on which food offerings can be placed. (The Greeks liked to offer bread or other grain-based foods to the gods; they are left on the altar for a day or so and then eaten reverently, having been blessed by the goddess.) All these are standard elements of classic Pagan Greek Neoplatonist practice, and as Athena was much revered by the Neoplatonists, she's likely to find this approach to her taste.

As for what to say, if you go to the DuckDuckGo search engine and type in the search string "prayers to Athena," you'll get quite a few hits. Athena's a popular goddess these days, just as she was in ancient times, and as you'd expect, she gives her votaries the ability to think clearly and write good prayers.

I'm not worried, because I know I'm going where She wants me to go, and I'm ready to put in some good hard desk-work (I just wish my local research library would open again, so I could use interlibrary loan and get easy photocopies of articles from printed scholarly journals; I didn't know how good I had it!), and I know my next step (start trawling classical studies scholarly journals that have been digitally indexed), but any advice or clues much appreciated from anyone in the forum!

replies.content:
I find Proclus' Hymn to Athene to be incredibly moving and beautiful. As head of Plato's Academy for much of the 5th century CE, Proclus had an intense devotion to Athene, and it shows.

I use a slightly-edited** version of R.M. van den Berg's translation, on pages 277-278 of his book Proclus' Hymns. If you can get access to a copy of this massively over-priced academic volume, there's a lot of good material in van den Berg's commentary as well.

I'm pretty sure that Thomas Taylor also did a translation, which a little work with Duckduckgo should turn up.

** Slightly edited, simply to delete a couple of petitions that mention things I personally don't want to ask her (or anyone else) for.

I've been in a couple of "magic" rituals in my Lutheran church.

In our church building, we have an altar with two candles, which are solemnly lit as the service begins, and solemnly extinguished at the end. If these acts aren't willful adjustments to consciousness, then I don't understand magic at all. The sacred time is indicated by these lights.

We're "gathering" to worship via Zoom these days, about 100 of us. I check in a few minutes early, and solemnly light a candle, in my home, but visible on my camera to all. The Pastor also (usually) lights a candle or two, whether at home or in the big empty church.

I was the President of the congregation when our female Pastor was "called" (which is church language for "hired"), and stood up to represent the congregation for a bit of liturgy in which she promised to teach and lead, and we promised to support and follow. We both are married, but visitors to the church have asked whether we are married to each other! There's something about that bit of liturgy that created some altered consciousness that people who don't know about our actual families can perceive.

Within the last five years or so, the Holy Communion (consuming tokens of bread and wine) has become a weekly occurrence, not a special occasion as in some Protestant denominations.

So, what OTHER ideas do you have about possible Protestant magic? Of course, we conduct baptisms, weddings, and funerals. (Privately referred to as "hatch, match, and dispatch". ;-) )

Lathechuck

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's much simpler than you make it out to be. You don't return to the center at any point between the first Rite of the Rays and the point where you've finished drawing things in the air. You leave the center, go to the eastern edge of the space where you're doing the working, and draw a pentagram.  Then, without going back to the center, you draw a line as you walk a quarter circle to the south, and draw a pentagram there. You continue in the same way, walking around the circumference of the space, until you're back in the east. Then you return to the center. The circumference doesn't have to be small, by the way; in temple ritual it's as big as the room.

comments.title: Magic as healing
comments.content:
Hello John. My question this week is on the use of magic as healing. I have often heard you quote the maxim "the planes are discrete and non-continous". If I'm understanding this correctly, it implies that physical effects require physical action, and magic's role - at least astral magic - is to act more as a key to unlock potentials within the mind, which may indirectly cause physical effects by leading to the magician and/or other intelligences around him/her to make more intelligent choices on the physical plane, but can never do so directly.

However, having read Regardie's 'Art of True Healing', he seems to imply that Middle Pillar visualisations can directly affect the physical health of the body. Do you believe he is mistaken or is there something subtle that I am missing here?

I am particularly curious because having practised the MP for several months now, I have often experienced a very visceral sensation of vital energy circulating through my body in the immediate aftermath. If I had to guess I would say this is etheric activity. How is this possible if the planes are discrete and non-continous?

How also would there be a mechanism for a healing modality like Acupuncture which, unless I'm mistaken, works primarily with with the etheric body, and yet is often reported to directly (i.e. without any further action apparently having occurred on the physical plane) affect physical health?

replies.title: Re: Magic as healing
replies.content: "The planes are discrete" does not mean "the planes are hermetically sealed from one another and no influence can pass between them." It means that forces on one plane can influence another plane only in specific, somewhat limited ways. Obviously the human body is a context in which such influences happen -- you can move your hand (which is material) in response to a thought (which is astral), after all -- and so visualizations can affect the material body in other ways, too. That effect is limited, however, which is why your visualizations won't make a table fly up into the air...

Exactly what are the specific ways in which one plane can influence another is something I look forward to pondering; I suspect the schema that divides the ten Sephiroth into five groups corresponding to the planes will provide plenty of clues.

comments.content: What would you say are the biggest pitfalls in studying astrology you see people fall into?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I've noted three major pitfalls. The first is the "Oh no, I'm doomed!" reaction, in which every negative indication gets blown wildly out of proportion -- you've got Mars in the 8th, therefore you're going to be murdered, or what have you. That's common among complete beginners, and you also see it with Tarot: "I just drew the Death card! I must be about to die!" et cetera, ad nauseam.

The second is the "I am a puppet and the stars are the strings" reaction, the idea that if something's in your horoscope or an upcoming transit or whatever, you can do nothing about it. That's not true at all; there's a lot of wiggle room.

Finally, you get the opposite mistake from these two, which is the "The stars do what I tell them" attitude. You've got wiggle room, but it's not infinite; if you've got an afflicted Venus, you're going to have some relationship trouble to deal with, even if you can finally work through it and get your love life on an even keel. comments.content:
Dear JMG,
Already this week, and on several earlier occasions, people have been talking about the madness afflicting what they describe as « the Left . » As an old Leftist myself, I wanted to comment on this because I think what is going on may be explicable, symbolically or actually, in magical terms, albeit not those normally used here.
Briefly (because this is not a political essay) what people call the « Left » today, has nothing to do with the collectivist, universalist, progressive mass movements of the last two hundred years, linked to trades unions, and civil society and community organisations. Most of the mass political parties established during this time still exist today, but in name only. Few if any of their current leaders claim to be of « the Left », or to use the traditional vocabulary of class-based struggle.
After the 1980s, shaken by the electoral success of Reagan and Thatcher, a new generation of party leaders abandoned any hope of recapturing power, and decided to reconfigure their parties for elite groups, accepting neoliberal economic polices as inevitable, and basing their ideology, inasmuch as they had one, on extreme social liberalism. Actual leftists were persecuted and driven out, as often before in history: liberals have always been the worst enemies of the Left. This produced parties with extreme neoliberal economic and social ideologies, developed for, and run by, the comfortably off with vaguely liberal inclinations. But of course these parties wanted to get elected, and so had to find a way of appealing to ordinary people. At first they retained some trappings of the Left, but progressively just kept the names. They effectively took the votes of ordinary people for granted, with implied slogans like, You Have no Choice, If You Think We Are Bad Look at the Opposition, and more recently, Vote for Us It’s Your Duty.
This worked for a while, but was bound to fall apart one day, as ordinary people began to awake from the magical trance into which they had been cast. Now, ordinary people have started to vote in large numbers for parties of the radical Right (and to some extent Left), Greens, and ethnic and local parties - or just not vote at all. Panic has set in, and this political blob is melting down, and starting to eat itself.
The above analysis is fairly standard on the genuine Left. My point here is that, whilst, it’s possible to use the vocabulary of commerce to explain what’s happened (think, a Private Equity buyout of a company) it’s also possible to use a magical one. After all, a group have taken over (many would say) the very soul of traditional Leftist parties, to the point that they now « possess » them. They use the glamour of advertising and PR to deceive and conceal who they really are, and they have done so in bad faith lying incessantly to themselves and each other. But because at the end of the day this was all about power and money, rather than any kind of coherent ideology, with the threat of genuinely using power they are starting to come apart and turning on each other. I’m sure there are plenty of magical and mythological precedents.
This seems to me a reasonable explanation, but I’d be interested to know what you think.

Nuclear power was supposed to produce energy too cheap to meter, psychoanalysis was supposed to completely transform human society, etc. In exactly the same way, Pluto itself was predicted to be much larger than the Earth; when it was discovered, it was estimated to be about Earth's size; each subsequent estimate brought in a smaller size, until by the time it was demoted, it was a tiny body roughly 1/400th of Earth's mass. I'd suggest that the same thing is true of advertising. It was never as effective as claimed -- can you think of heavily advertised products that bombed? I certainly can -- and at this point it seems likely to me that the whole thing will turn out to be fantastically overhyped.

comments.title: emotional currents
comments.content:
Archdruid,

So I've noticed a serious pitfall in determining emotions that start within and those that come from outside. It is very easy for a mage to say that something their feeling is the result of a mentative wave from the outside. i.e. "this sudden anger is very uncharacteristic of me, thus it must be from an external source." It seems to me that in order to clearly distinguish between external vs. internal currents one must first accept that one is capable of a range of reactions to any event, even if one doesn't accept that one can react to an event in a specific way, or doesn't like their own reaction to an event, all the reactions are possible.

A magical practitioner that doesn't accept their capacity for anger, fear, or any other emotion will be more likely to pin the blame on external influences. In most cases, it is better to own the currents, since that allows you to perform mental alchemy.

Is this accurate?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: emotional currents
replies.content: Yes, but it's not just a matter of "am I capable of this?"  Emotions aren't just generic anger, generic fear, or what have you; any emotion will have a particular flavor shaped by the mind in which it originated and the circumstances and influences that generated it. That being the case, it's quite possible to say to oneself, "I know that I'm perfectly capable of being this angry, but this specific anger doesn't feel like mine" -- and in that case, given some psychic abilities or a talent for divination, you can often figure out whose it is, and do something helpful about it.

Can the same be said for mentative currents? That mentative energies are only partially produced by self and, like breathing, we inhale and exhale the energies. Thus if we inhale the anger, our experience of that anger is shaped by our own experiences?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Different classes of spiritual beings have their own very distinctive feel, and with a little experience it's not hard to learn to recognize their presence. In my experience, for example, angels bring a sense of opening outward into a kind of luminous silence; the universe seems vaster, brighter, and more hushed wherever they manifest. The gods and goddesses of the Pagan faiths make the world more vivid and brightly colored; scents and tastes and sounds are all stronger, and everything seems more vital, more full of life and strength and joy. Planetary intelligences bring a focused clarity, always tinged with the quality of their planet, and so on.

Demonic beings also have a distinctive feel. Where they are, everything feels hot, confused, unthinking, and yet there's also a quality I can only describe as "mechanical." It's harder to think around them, and the more they manifest, the more likely you are to see people falling into rigid, automatic, mechanical patterns of thought and behavior weirdly disconnected from any kind of context. That's what we've seen since Trump broke free of the pack in the runup to 2016; the Left has been caught in a whole series of bizarre, repetitive patterns of thought and action.

The blindingly obvious, necessary steps after their 2016 defeat -- find out what they did that alienated their former voters in the upper Midwest, and stop doing it -- were exactly those things the Left angrily refused to do, and their rhetoric very quickly took on that hot, confused, unthinking quality I've just described. Watch the way everything turns into a reason to scream "Orange Man Bad!" irrespective of mere reason, and you can see the typical qualities of demonic influence on human beings. Do you recall the way the Left swung from #BelieveAllWomen to #BelieveJoeBiden in an eyeblink, or from denouncing Trump as a racist for quarantining coronavirus patients to denouncing Trump as a racist for not quarantining them fast enough? That's the kind of thing I mean.

1) You mention in your "Encyclopedia of Natural Magic" that rubies are best worn on the left hand. Is there a known reason for this, or is it just one of those things the texts say?

2) Do you know of any discussion of differing effects from wearing rings on different fingers? It feels like it makes a difference to me, but I'm not quite sensitive enough yet to figure out what the difference is.

Thanks, as always!
-Peter

replies.title: Re: Natural magic
replies.content:
1) The left hand is supposed to have an energetic connection to the heart, and so having a ruby on that hand strengthens the heart.

2) No, but it's definitely a field in which serious research could be done.
